content:
# Exodus Chapter 16 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~41r–42v (PDF pp. 79–80); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 57–58 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_016_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_016_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_016_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_016_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Wilderness of Sin: Murmuring Against Moses and Aaron (vv. 1–3)

**1** And they journeyed [**vayyis'u**] from Elim, and all the congregation [**kol-adat**] of the sons of Israel came to the wilderness of Sin [**midbar-Sin**], which is between Elim and Sinai [**Sinai**], on the fifteenth day [**bachamishah asar yom**] of the second month [**lachodesh hasheni**] after their going out [**letsetam**] from the land of Egypt.

**2** And all the congregation of the sons of Israel murmured [**vayyillonu**] against Moses and against Aaron in the wilderness.

**3** And the sons of Israel said to them, "Would that we had died [**mi-yitten mutenu**] by the hand of the LORD in the land of Egypt, when we sat [**beshivtenu**] by the pot of meat [**al-sir habasar**], when we ate bread [**be'okhlenu lechem**] to the full [**lasova**]. For you have brought us out [**hotsetem**] into this wilderness to kill [**lehamit**] all this assembly [**et-kol-haqqahal hazeh**] with hunger [**bara'av**]."

## The LORD Promises Bread from Heaven (vv. 4–8)

**4** And the LORD said to Moses, "Behold, I am about to rain [**mamtir**] bread [**lechem**] from heaven [**min-hashamayim**] for you. And the people shall go out and gather [**velaqetu**] the matter of a day in its day [**devar-yom beyomo**], so that I may test them [**lema'an anassenu**], whether they will walk [**hayelekhu**] in My instruction [**betorati**] or not.

**5** And it shall be on the sixth day [**bayyom hashishi**], that they shall prepare [**vehekhinu**] what they bring in [**et asher yavi'u**], and it shall be double [**mishneh**] over what they gather [**al asher-yilqetu**] day by day [**yom yom**]."

**6** And Moses and Aaron said to all the sons of Israel, "At evening [**erev**] you shall know [**vida'tem**] that the LORD brought you out [**hotsi etkhem**] from the land of Egypt.

**7** And in the morning [**uvoqer**] you shall see [**ure'item**] the glory [**et-kevod**] of the LORD, in that He hears [**beshom'o**] your murmurings [**et-telunoteikhem**] against the LORD. And what are we [**venachnu mah**], that you murmur [**tallinu**] against us?"

**8** And Moses said, "When the LORD gives [**betet**] you in the evening meat [**basar**] to eat, and bread in the morning to the full [**lasova**] — in that the LORD hears [**bishmo'a**] your murmurings [**et-telunoteikhem**] which you murmur [**mallinom**] against Him — and what are we [**venachnu mah**]? Not against us [**lo alenu**] are your murmurings, but against the LORD [**ki al-YHWH**]."

## The Glory of the LORD Appears (vv. 9–12)

**9** And Moses said to Aaron, "Say to all the congregation of the sons of Israel, 'Draw near [**qirvu**] before the LORD [**lifney YHWH**], for He has heard [**shama**] your murmurings [**et-telunoteikhem**].'"

**10** And it came to pass, as Aaron spoke to all the congregation of the sons of Israel, that they turned [**vayyifnu**] toward the wilderness, and behold, the glory of the LORD [**kevod YHWH**] appeared [**nir'ah**] in the cloud [**be'anan**].

**11** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying,

**12** "I have heard [**shamati**] the murmurings [**et-telunnot**] of the sons of Israel. Speak to them, saying, 'Between the evenings [**ben ha'arbayim**] you shall eat meat [**basar**], and in the morning [**uvabboqer**] you shall be filled [**tisbe'u**] with bread [**lachem**], and you shall know [**vida'tem**] that I am the LORD your God [**ki ani YHWH Eloheikhem**].'"

## Quail and Manna: The Provision (vv. 13–15)

**13** And it came to pass in the evening [**ba'erev**] that the quail [**hasselav**] came up [**vatta'al**] and covered [**vatekhass**] the camp [**et-hamachaneh**]. And in the morning [**uvabboqer**] there was a layer [**shikhat**] of dew [**hattal**] around the camp [**seviv lamachaneh**].

**14** And the layer of dew went up [**vatta'al**], and behold, upon the face [**al-peney**] of the wilderness, a fine [**daq**], flaky thing [**mechuspas**], fine as frost [**daq kakkefor**] upon the ground [**al-ha'arets**].

**15** And the sons of Israel saw it, and they said each man to his brother [**ish el-achiv**], "What is it? [**man hu**]" — for they did not know [**lo yade'u**] what it was [**mah-hu**]. And Moses said to them, "It is the bread [**hu halleckem**] which the LORD has given [**natan**] to you to eat [**le'okhlah**]."

## Instructions for Gathering: An Omer per Head (vv. 16–18)

**16** "This is the thing [**zeh haddavar**] which the LORD has commanded [**tsivvah**]: 'Gather [**liqtu**] of it, each man according to his eating [**ish lefi okhlo**] — an omer [**omer**] per head [**lagulgoleth**], according to the number of your persons [**mispar nafshoteikhem**]. Each man for those who are in his tent [**ish la'asher be'oholo**] shall you take [**tiqqachu**].'"

**17** And the sons of Israel did so [**vayyya'asu khen**], and they gathered [**vayyilqetu**], some more [**hammarbeh**] and some less [**vehamma'it**].

**18** And they measured [**vayyamoddu**] with the omer [**ba'omer**], and the one who gathered much [**hammarbeh**] had nothing over [**lo he'dif**], and the one who gathered little [**vehamma'it**] had no lack [**lo hechsir**]. Each man according to his eating [**ish lefi okhlo**] they gathered [**laqatu**].

## The Prohibition Against Keeping Manna Overnight (vv. 19–21)

**19** And Moses said to them, "Let no man leave any of it [**al-yoter ish mimmennu**] until morning [**ad-boqer**]."

**20** And they did not listen [**velo-shame'u**] to Moses, and some men left some of it [**vayyotiru anashim mimmennu**] until morning, and it bred worms [**vayyarum tolaim**] and it stank [**vayiv'ash**]. And Moses was angry [**vayyiqtsof**] with them.

**21** And they gathered it [**vayyilqetuhu**] morning by morning [**babboqer babboqer**], each man according to his eating. And when the sun grew hot [**vecham hashemesh**], it melted [**venamas**].

## The Double Portion and the Sabbath (vv. 22–26)

**22** And it came to pass on the sixth day [**bayyom hashishi**], they gathered double bread [**lechem mishneh**] — two omers [**sheney ha'omer**] for the one [**la'echad**]. And all the leaders [**kol-nesi'ey**] of the congregation came and told [**vayyaggidu**] Moses.

**23** And he said to them, "This is what the LORD has spoken [**hu asher dibber YHWH**]: 'A rest [**shabbaton**], a holy Sabbath [**shabbat-qodesh**] to the LORD [**laYHWH**] is tomorrow [**machar**]. What you will bake [**et asher-tofu**], bake, and what you will boil [**et asher-tevashelu**], boil. And all that is left over [**kol-ha'oddef**], set aside [**hannichu**] for yourselves as a keeping [**lemishmeret**] until the morning [**ad-habboqer**].'"

**24** And they set it aside [**vayyanichu**] until the morning, as Moses had commanded [**ka'asher tsivvah Mosheh**]. And it did not stink [**velo hiv'ish**], and no worm [**veriammah**] was in it.

**25** And Moses said, "Eat it [**ikhluhu**] today [**hayyom**], for today is a Sabbath [**shabbat**] to the LORD. Today you will not find it [**lo timtsa'uhu**] in the field [**bassadeh**].

**26** Six days [**sheshet yamim**] you shall gather it, and on the seventh day [**uvayyom hashevi'i**] is a Sabbath [**shabbat**] — there shall be none in it [**lo yihyeh-bo**]."

## Disobedience on the Sabbath (vv. 27–30)

**27** And it came to pass on the seventh day, some of the people [**min-ha'am**] went out to gather [**lilqot**], and they did not find [**velo matsa'u**].

**28** And the LORD said to Moses, "How long [**ad-anah**] do you refuse [**me'antem**] to keep [**lishmor**] My commandments [**mitsvotay**] and My instructions [**vetorotay**]?"

**29** "See [**re'u**] that the LORD has given [**natan**] you the Sabbath [**hashabbat**]. Therefore [**al-ken**] He gives [**noten**] you on the sixth day bread of two days [**lechem yomayim**]. Remain [**shevu**], each man in his place [**ish tachtav**]; let no man go out [**al-yetse ish**] from his place [**mimqomo**] on the seventh day."

**30** And the people rested [**vayyishbetu**] on the seventh day.

## The Name and Description of Manna (vv. 31)

**31** And the house of Israel [**beyt-Yisra'el**] called [**vayyiqre'u**] its name "manna" [**man**]. And it was like seed of coriander [**kizra-gad**], white [**lavan**], and its taste [**veta'mo**] was like a wafer [**ketsappichit**] with honey [**bidevash**].

## The Command to Preserve an Omer (vv. 32–34)

**32** And Moses said, "This is the thing which the LORD has commanded: 'A full omer [**melo ha'omer**] of it is to be kept [**lemishmeret**] for your generations [**ledoroteikhem**], so that they may see [**lema'an yir'u**] the bread which I fed you [**he'ekhalti etkhem**] in the wilderness, when I brought you out [**behotsi'i etkhem**] from the land of Egypt.'"

**33** And Moses said to Aaron, "Take [**qach**] one jar [**tsintseneth achat**] and put [**veten**] there a full omer [**melo-ha'omer**] of manna [**man**], and set it down [**vehannach**] before the LORD [**lifney YHWH**], as a keeping [**lemishmeret**] for your generations [**ledoroteikhem**]."

**34** As the LORD commanded [**tsivvah**] Moses, so Aaron set it down [**vayyannichemu**] before the Testimony [**lifney ha'Edut**], as a keeping [**lemishmaret**].

## The Duration and the Measure (vv. 35–36)

**35** And the sons of Israel ate [**akhlu**] the manna [**et-hamman**] forty years [**arba'im shanah**], until they came [**ad-bo'am**] to a land that was inhabited [**el-erets noshavet**]. The manna they ate [**et-hamman akhlu**] until they came [**ad-bo'am**] to the border [**el-qetseh**] of the land of Canaan [**erets Kena'an**].

**36** And the omer [**veha'omer**] is a tenth [**asirit**] of the ephah [**ha'efah**].

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations
The Leningrad Codex folios ~41r–42v (PDF pp. 79–80) contain the entirety of Exodus 16. The text appears in the standard three-column prose layout, without any special scribal formatting — this is narrative text, not poetry, and is written accordingly. Page 79 (the first image) contains the end of Exodus 15 at the top of the page and transitions into Exodus 16. The opening of chapter 16 is visible in the lower portion of the page, beginning with the departure from Elim (vayyis'u me'Elim). Page 80 (the second image) continues with the bulk of the chapter in a three-column layout, carrying the narrative through the manna instructions, the Sabbath institution, and the concluding verses about the preservation of an omer and the duration of the manna.

A petuchah (open paragraph) marker appears before 16:1, marking the transition from the Elim oasis (15:27) to the wilderness of Sin — a major narrative shift from provision to deprivation, from rest to journey. A setumah (closed paragraph) marker appears before v. 4, separating the people's murmuring from the LORD's response — a division that highlights the shift from human complaint to divine speech. A setumah appears before v. 6, marking Moses and Aaron's address to the people. A setumah marker appears before v. 9, where Moses instructs Aaron to address the congregation. A setumah appears before v. 11, where the LORD speaks directly to Moses. A setumah appears before v. 13, marking the transition from divine speech to narrative fulfillment — the actual appearance of quail and manna. A setumah appears before v. 16, introducing the specific gathering instructions. A setumah marker appears before v. 19, where Moses gives the prohibition against overnight keeping. A setumah appears before v. 22, introducing the Sabbath double-portion episode. A setumah appears before v. 28, marking the LORD's rebuke. A petuchah appears before v. 32, marking the transition to the command to preserve manna for future generations — a shift from immediate provision to memorial institution. A setumah appears before v. 35, introducing the summary note about the forty years of manna.

The masorah parva annotations are visible throughout the folios. Annotations appear on midbar-Sin (v. 1, "wilderness of Sin" — distinguishing this from midbar-Sinai and midbar-Tsin), on vayyillonu (v. 2, "murmured" — the murmuring verb that becomes a leitmotif of the wilderness narrative), on man hu (v. 15, "what is it" — the folk etymology of manna), on omer (v. 16, the unit of measure — noting its definition in v. 36), on shabbaton shabbat-qodesh (v. 23, the first use of this compound Sabbath expression in the Torah), on torotay (v. 28, "My instructions" — the plural form of torah), on tsintseneth (v. 33, "jar" — a hapax legomenon in the Hebrew Bible), and on ha'Edut (v. 34, "the Testimony" — an anachronistic reference to the Ark that does not yet exist in narrative time). The cantillation on v. 4 places emphasis on hineni mamtir lakhem lechem min-hashamayim ("Behold, I am about to rain bread from heaven for you"), with the etnachta creating a pause before the testing clause. The cantillation on v. 15 gives distinctive emphasis to man hu ("what is it?"), marking the etymological moment. The cantillation on v. 29 places emphasis on re'u ki-YHWH natan lakhem hashabbat ("See, the LORD has given you the Sabbath"), marking this as a theologically weighty declaration.

The Codex Vaticanus pages 57–58 preserve the LXX text of Exodus 16. The text begins on page 57 in the three-column continuous uncial script characteristic of the codex. At v. 1, the LXX renders midbar-Sin as tēn erēmon Sin ("the wilderness of Sin"), transliterating the Hebrew place name. At v. 3, the LXX renders mi-yitten mutenu ("would that we had died") as ophelen apethanomen ("would that we had died"), using the optative construction with ophelon. The phrase al-sir habasar ("by the pot of meat") is rendered epi tōn lebētōn tou kreōs ("over the pots of meat"), using the plural lebētes ("cauldrons, pots"). At v. 4, the LXX renders mamtir lakhem lechem min-hashamayim as egō huō humin artous ek tou ouranou ("I rain for you bread/loaves from heaven"), using the verb huō ("to rain") and artous (plural of artos, "bread, loaf"). At v. 7, the LXX renders kevod YHWH as tēn doxan kuriou ("the glory of the Lord"), using doxa for kavod. At v. 13, the LXX renders hasselav as ortugometra ("quail"), the standard Greek term for the quail (Coturnix coturnix). At v. 14, the LXX renders mechuspas ("flaky, scale-like") as leptom hōsei koriannon leukon ("fine like white coriander"), apparently conflating elements from v. 14 and v. 31. At v. 15, the LXX renders man hu as ti estin touto ("what is this?"), translating the expression rather than transliterating it, and then adds ou gar ēdeisan ti ēn ("for they did not know what it was"). At v. 16, the LXX renders omer lagulgoleth as gomor epi kephalēn ("a gomor per head"), transliterating omer as gomor. At v. 23, the LXX renders shabbaton shabbat-qodesh as sabbata anapausis hagia ("Sabbaths, a holy rest"), using the plural sabbata and adding anapausis ("rest, cessation"). At v. 29, the LXX's rendering of re'u ki-YHWH natan lakhem hashabbat as idete ho gar kurios edōken humin tēn hēmeran tautēn tēn sabbatōn ("see, for the Lord has given you this day of the Sabbaths") adds hēmeran tautēn ("this day"), making the temporal reference more explicit. At v. 31, the LXX renders man as man (transliteration) and kizra-gad as hōsei sperma koriannou leukon ("like seed of white coriander"), rendering gad as koriannon ("coriander"). At v. 33, the LXX renders tsintseneth as stamnon chrusoun ("a golden jar"), adding the adjective chrusous ("golden") — a detail not present in the Hebrew text but reflected also in Hebrews 9:4. At v. 34, the LXX renders lifney ha'Edut as enantion tou marturiou ("before the Testimony"), using marturion for Edut. At v. 36, the LXX renders the omer-ephah equivalence as to de gomor to dekaton tōn triōn metrōn ēn ("the gomor was a tenth of the three measures"), interpreting the ephah as consisting of three seahs.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"Would that we had died" (v. 3) — mi-yitten mutenu:** Hebrew mi-yitten mutenu beyad-YHWH be'erets Mitsrayim beshivtenu al-sir habasar be'okhlenu lechem lasova ki-hotsetem otanu el-hammidbar hazeh lehamit et-kol-haqqahal hazeh bara'av. The optative construction mi-yitten ("who will give?" = "would that...") followed by the infinitive construct mutenu ("our dying") expresses an unrealizable wish — the people wish they had died in Egypt rather than face starvation in the wilderness. This is the first full murmuring speech in Exodus (the verb vayyillonu appeared at 15:24 but with only a brief question). The content of the wish is shocking: they idealize Egyptian slavery, remembering sir habasar ("the pot of meat" — literally "the pot of the flesh") and lechem lasova ("bread to the full"). The noun sir (plural sirot) is a large cooking pot or cauldron; the construct sir habasar evokes a communal cooking pot full of meat. Whether the Israelites actually had abundant meat in Egypt is beside the point — memory has been rewritten by hunger. The verb hotsetem ("you brought out," Hiphil of yatsa', 2mp) addresses Moses and Aaron directly and accusatively: "you brought us out" — as though the Exodus were Moses' idea rather than God's command. The purpose clause lehamit et-kol-haqqahal hazeh bara'av ("to kill all this assembly with hunger") accuses Moses and Aaron of deliberate murder. The word qahal ("assembly, congregation") is a formal term for the gathered community — the murmuring is not private but public, institutional, corporate. LOGOS renders "Would that we had died" for mi-yitten mutenu and "the pot of meat" for sir habasar. The LXX renders ophelen apethanomen plēgentes hupo kuriou en gē Aiguptou ("would that we had died, struck by the Lord in the land of Egypt"), adding plēgentes ("struck, smitten") — the people wish for death by divine plague in Egypt, making the death wish more explicit and more theological.

2. **"I am about to rain bread from heaven" (v. 4) — hineni mamtir lakhem lechem min-hashamayim:** Hebrew vayyomer YHWH el-Mosheh hineni mamtir lakhem lechem min-hashamayim veyatsa ha'am velaqtu devar-yom beyomo lema'an anassenu hayelekhu betorati im-lo. The divine response to the murmuring is remarkable: instead of anger, provision. The Hiphil participle mamtir ("causing to rain," from matar, "to rain") presents God as the source of the heavenly rain — the same God who controls the waters of the Sea now controls the bread that falls from the sky. The phrase lechem min-hashamayim ("bread from heaven") becomes one of the defining images of the wilderness period and is cited extensively in later tradition (Neh. 9:15; Ps. 78:24; Ps. 105:40; John 6:31–32). The bread is from hashamayim ("the heavens") — its origin is divine, not terrestrial. The clause devar-yom beyomo ("the matter/portion of a day in its day") establishes the daily rhythm: each day's portion is gathered on that day and no other. This is not merely a logistical instruction but a spiritual discipline — dependence on God is to be renewed daily, not stored up. The purpose clause lema'an anassenu ("so that I may test them," Piel of nasah) reveals that the manna is simultaneously provision and test. The test is specified: hayelekhu betorati im-lo ("whether they will walk in My instruction or not"). The noun torah here means "instruction" in its broadest sense — the entire framework of divine teaching, not yet codified at Sinai but already operative. LOGOS renders "I am about to rain" for hineni mamtir and "the matter of a day in its day" for devar-yom beyomo. The LXX renders idou egō huō humin artous ek tou ouranou ("behold, I rain for you bread from heaven"), using the present tense huō rather than a future or imminent construction.

3. **"What is it? — man hu" (v. 15) — the etymology of manna:** Hebrew vayyir'u veney-Yisra'el vayyomeru ish el-achiv man hu ki lo yade'u mah-hu vayyomer Mosheh alehem hu halleckem asher natan YHWH lakhem le'okhlah. The expression man hu is the folk etymology for the manna. The word man is most likely an Aramaic/dialectal interrogative particle meaning "what?" — thus man hu = "what is it?" This is confirmed by the explanatory clause that follows: ki lo yade'u mah-hu ("for they did not know what it was"), where the standard Hebrew mah replaces the dialectal man. The name "manna" (man) thus encodes perpetual bewilderment — it is the food whose name is a question. Some scholars connect man to the Egyptian mennu or to an Arabic cognate mann (a sweet exudation from the tamarisk tree, Tamarix gallica, still found in the Sinai Peninsula), but the text itself explains the name as arising from the question man hu. The description in v. 14 — daq mechuspas daq kakkefor al-ha'arets ("a fine, flaky thing, fine as frost upon the ground") — uses two rare words: daq ("fine, thin, small" — used of fine dust and thin cakes) and mechuspas (a hapax legomenon, possibly meaning "flaked, scaled, peeled" — from a root ch-s-p meaning "to strip, to peel"). The simile kakkefor ("like the frost") compares the manna's appearance to the white crystalline layer of frost. LOGOS renders "What is it?" for man hu, preserving the interrogative force. The LXX renders ti estin touto ("what is this?"), translating rather than transliterating.

4. **"An omer per head" (v. 16) — omer lagulgoleth:** Hebrew liqtu mimmennu ish lefi okhlo omer lagulgoleth mispar nafshoteikhem ish la'asher be'oholo tiqqachu. The omer is a dry measure defined in v. 36 as a tenth of an ephah (approximately 2.3 liters or about 2 quarts). The word lagulgoleth ("per head," literally "per skull," from gulgoleth, "skull, head") is a census term — the same word used in Numbers 1:2 for the counting of the people (the English word "Golgotha" derives from this root via Aramaic). The instruction is that each person gathers one omer per skull — per individual. The phrase ish lefi okhlo ("each man according to his eating," literally "a man according to the mouth of his eating") uses the idiom lefi ("according to the mouth of") to mean "in proportion to." The instruction ish la'asher be'oholo ("each man for those who are in his tent") extends the responsibility to the household: the head of household gathers for all who dwell in his tent. This is communal provision organized through household units. LOGOS renders "an omer per head" for omer lagulgoleth. The LXX renders gomor epi kephalēn ("a gomor per head"), transliterating omer as gomor and translating lagulgoleth as epi kephalēn ("upon/per head").

5. **"A rest, a holy Sabbath to the LORD" (v. 23) — shabbaton shabbat-qodesh laYHWH:** Hebrew hu asher dibber YHWH shabbaton shabbat-qodesh laYHWH machar et asher-tofu efu ve'et kol-asher-tevashelu bashelu ve'et kol-ha'odef hanichu lakhem lemishmeret ad-habboqer. This is the first explicit Sabbath legislation in the Torah — preceding Sinai and the Decalogue. The word shabbaton (an intensive or abstract noun from the root sh-b-t, "to cease, to rest") denotes a complete cessation, an emphatic rest. It is paired with shabbat-qodesh ("a holy Sabbath" or "a Sabbath of holiness"), creating a double emphasis: rest-of-rest, holy cessation. The phrase laYHWH ("to the LORD") dedicates the Sabbath — the rest belongs to God, not merely to the people. The practical instructions that follow — efu ("bake," from afah) and bashelu ("boil," from bashal) — indicate that the manna could be prepared in multiple ways: baked or boiled. The verb hanichu ("set aside, lay down," Hiphil of nuach, "to rest") uses the same root as the noun menukhah ("rest") — the surplus food is "rested" for the next day. The phrase lemishmeret ("for a keeping, for a safeguard," from shamar, "to keep, to guard") uses the same word that will later describe priestly duty and covenant obligation. The manna that is "kept" on Friday evening does not rot (v. 24), unlike manna kept on other nights (v. 20) — the Sabbath transforms the nature of the food. LOGOS renders "a rest, a holy Sabbath to the LORD" for shabbaton shabbat-qodesh laYHWH. The LXX renders sabbata anapausis hagia tō kuriō ("Sabbaths, a holy rest to the Lord"), using the plural sabbata.

6. **"See, the LORD has given you the Sabbath" (v. 29) — re'u ki-YHWH natan lakhem hashabbat:** Hebrew re'u ki-YHWH natan lakhem hashabbat al-ken hu noten lakhem bayyom hashishi lechem yomayim shevu ish tachtav al-yetse ish mimqomo bayyom hashevi'i. The imperative re'u ("see!" plural) commands perception — the Sabbath is something to be seen, recognized, grasped. The verb natan ("has given") presents the Sabbath as a gift: God gives the Sabbath to Israel, not as a burden but as a bestowment. The phrase al-ken hu noten lakhem bayyom hashishi lechem yomayim ("therefore He gives you on the sixth day bread of two days") explains the double portion of Friday as evidence of the Sabbath gift — the sixth day's abundance is the proof of the seventh day's rest. The command shevu ish tachtav ("remain, each man in his place") uses the verb shavu (imperative of yashav, "to sit, to dwell, to remain") — on the Sabbath, one stays where one is. The phrase ish tachtav ("each man under himself" = "in his place") and the prohibition al-yetse ish mimqomo ("let no man go out from his place") establish the principle of Sabbath rest as remaining in place. This verse becomes the basis for the rabbinic concept of techum Shabbat ("Sabbath boundary") — the limit beyond which one may not walk on the Sabbath. LOGOS renders "See, the LORD has given you the Sabbath" literally. The LXX adds hēmeran tautēn ("this day"), making the demonstrative more concrete.

7. **"Manna" (v. 31) — man; and its description: kizra-gad lavan veta'mo ketsappichit bidevash:** Hebrew vayyiqre'u veyt-Yisra'el et-shemo man vehu kizra-gad lavan veta'mo ketsappichit bidevash. The name man is formalized here as the official designation: the house of Israel "called its name man." The description that follows provides two characteristics: appearance and taste. The appearance is kizra-gad lavan ("like the seed of coriander, white"). The noun gad is identified as coriander (Coriandrum sativum), a plant whose small, round, whitish-yellow seeds would provide a familiar visual comparison. The adjective lavan ("white") specifies the color. The taste is ketsappichit bidevash ("like a wafer with honey"). The noun tsappichit (from tsapach, possibly meaning "to flatten, to spread thin") refers to a thin, flat cake or wafer. The phrase bidevash ("with honey") indicates that the manna tasted like a honey-wafer — sweet and delicate. Numbers 11:7–8 provides an alternative description: the manna is compared to bedolach (bdellium, a fragrant resin) in appearance and its taste is described as leshad hashamen ("like the taste of rich oil/cream"). The two descriptions are complementary rather than contradictory — one emphasizes sweetness, the other richness. LOGOS renders "like seed of coriander, white" for kizra-gad lavan and "like a wafer with honey" for ketsappichit bidevash. The LXX renders hōsei sperma koriannou leukon ("like seed of white coriander") and to de geuma autou hōs engris en meliti ("and its taste was like a cake/wafer in honey"), using engris (a type of cake) for tsappichit.

8. **"A jar" (v. 33) — tsintseneth:** Hebrew vayyomer Mosheh el-Aharon qach tsintseneth achat veten-shammah melo-ha'omer man vehannach oto lifney YHWH lemishmeret ledoroteikhem. The noun tsintseneth is a hapax legomenon — it occurs only here in the entire Hebrew Bible. Its etymology is uncertain; it may be related to an Egyptian or Semitic root for a container or flask. The lack of any further description in the Hebrew text leaves the material and shape entirely unspecified — it is simply "a jar" or "a container." The LXX, however, renders stamnon chrusoun ("a golden jar"), adding the adjective "golden" — a detail absent from the MT. This LXX addition is reflected in Hebrews 9:4, which describes the Ark's contents as including chrysēn stamnon echousan to manna ("a golden jar having the manna"). Whether the LXX reflects an older tradition or represents an interpretive expansion is debated. The command to place the jar lifney YHWH ("before the LORD") and the specification in v. 34 lifney ha'Edut ("before the Testimony") indicate that the manna-jar is to be placed before the Ark of the Testimony — an institution that does not yet exist in narrative time. This anachronism (the Testimony/Ark is not built until Exodus 25–40) indicates that the narrator is writing from a perspective in which the Ark already exists, or that the command is proleptically given for future execution. LOGOS renders "jar" for tsintseneth, preserving the Hebrew's lack of material specification. The LXX's "golden jar" is noted but not adopted into the formal translation.

9. **"The quail" (v. 13) — hasselav:** Hebrew vayehi va'erev vatta'al hasselav vatekhass et-hamachaneh. The noun selav (here with the article, hasselav) refers to the common quail (Coturnix coturnix), a migratory bird that passes through the Sinai Peninsula in large numbers during spring and autumn migrations. The quail are small, ground-nesting birds that fly low and in dense flocks, making them easy to catch when exhausted from migration — a detail consistent with the description of their "covering the camp." The verb vatta'al ("came up," from alah) suggests the quail arrived from a lower elevation or from the direction of the sea. The quail episode here is brief — a single verse (v. 13a) — compared to the extended account in Numbers 11:31–34, where the quail arrival leads to plague and the naming of Qivrot-Hata'avah ("Graves of Craving"). In Exodus 16, the quail are mentioned almost in passing; the theological focus is entirely on the manna. The verb vatekhass ("and it covered," from kasah) uses the same verb used for the Sea covering the Egyptians (14:28; 15:5, 10) — a verbal echo that links divine provision to divine judgment: the same God who covered the enemy with water covers the camp with food. LOGOS renders "quail" for selav. The LXX renders ortugometra ("quail"), the standard Hellenistic Greek term.

10. **"Before the Testimony" (v. 34) — lifney ha'Edut:** Hebrew ka'asher tsivvah YHWH el-Mosheh vayyannichemu Aharon lifney ha'Edut lemishmaret. The noun Edut ("Testimony") refers to the Tablets of the Testimony (luchot ha'Edut) and by extension to the Ark of the Testimony (aron ha'Edut) in which they are placed. The Edut is the covenant document — God's "testimony" or "attestation" — the Ten Commandments inscribed on the tablets. The placement of the manna-jar "before the Testimony" (later specified as within the Ark in the LXX and rabbinic tradition, or before/beside the Ark in other readings) creates a memorial link between daily provision and covenant law: the bread from heaven is preserved alongside the words from heaven. The verb vayyannichemu ("and he set it down," Hiphil of nuach with 3ms suffix, referring to the manna or the jar) describes Aaron's act of depositing the jar in its sacred location. As noted above, this is narratively proleptic — the Ark will not be constructed until Exodus 37. The narrator compresses time, placing the command in its logical theological context rather than its chronological one. LOGOS renders "the Testimony" for ha'Edut. The LXX renders tou marturiou ("of the Testimony"), using marturion, the standard Greek equivalent.

11. **"Forty years" (v. 35) — arba'im shanah:** Hebrew uveney Yisra'el akhlu et-hamman arba'im shanah ad-bo'am el-erets noshavet et-hamman akhlu ad-bo'am el-qetseh erets Kena'an. The number forty (arba'im) is the defining number of the wilderness period — forty years of wandering, forty years of manna. The repetition in this verse is emphatic: et-hamman akhlu ("the manna they ate") is stated twice, with two different endpoints: ad-bo'am el-erets noshavet ("until they came to a land that was inhabited") and ad-bo'am el-qetseh erets Kena'an ("until they came to the border of the land of Canaan"). The first phrase (erets noshavet, "an inhabited land") is general — any land with permanent settlement. The second phrase (qetseh erets Kena'an, "the border of the land of Canaan") is specific — the western edge of the promised land. Joshua 5:12 records the cessation of the manna: "the manna ceased on the day after they ate of the produce of the land." The manna thus spans the entire wilderness period from Exodus 16 to Joshua 5 — from the second month after leaving Egypt to the entry into Canaan. The phrase erets noshavet uses the Niphal participle of yashav ("to sit, to dwell, to inhabit") — a "dwelt-in land," a settled land, in contrast to the midbar ("wilderness") where no one permanently dwells. LOGOS renders "forty years" literally. The LXX renders tessarakonta etē ("forty years").

12. **"The omer is a tenth of the ephah" (v. 36) — veha'omer asirit ha'efah hu:** This final verse is a parenthetical editorial note explaining the omer measure for readers who may not be familiar with it. The ephah (efah) was the standard dry measure in ancient Israel, approximately 22 liters (roughly 3/5 of a bushel). A tenth of the ephah — the omer — would thus be approximately 2.2 liters. The omer as a unit of measure appears almost exclusively in Exodus 16 (vv. 16, 18, 22, 32, 33, 36); elsewhere the term omer refers to a sheaf of grain (as in Lev. 23:10–15, the omer offering). The editorial nature of this note — explaining an archaic measure — suggests that this verse was added by a later redactor or that the narrator anticipates an audience for whom the omer is no longer a commonly understood unit. LOGOS renders "a tenth of the ephah" for asirit ha'efah. The LXX renders to dekaton tōn triōn metrōn ("a tenth of the three measures"), interpreting the ephah as a three-seah measure.

| midbar-Sin | wilderness of Sin | v. 1; between Elim and Sinai; not midbar-Tsin (Zin) |
| bachamishah asar yom | on the fifteenth day | v. 1; precise date — one month after Passover |
| lachodesh hasheni | of the second month | v. 1; Iyyar in the later calendar |

| kizra-gad | like seed of coriander | v. 31; gad = coriander (Coriandrum sativum) |

| ketsappichit | like a wafer | v. 31; tsappichit = thin flat cake (from tsapach) |
| bidevash | with honey | v. 31; devash = "honey" — sweet taste |
| melo ha'omer | full omer | v. 32; the complete measure |

---

### Theological Note: Manna, Sabbath, and the Pedagogy of Dependence

Exodus 16 is one of the most theologically dense chapters in the Torah. It introduces three foundational themes — divine provision (manna), divine testing (torah-obedience), and divine rest (Sabbath) — and weaves them into a single narrative of Israel's education in the wilderness. The chapter is structured as a sequence of divine speeches and human responses, with each episode revealing a further dimension of what it means for a redeemed people to depend upon their Redeemer.

The murmuring of vv. 2–3 sets the theological problem. The people who sang "Who is like You among the gods?" (15:11) and "The LORD will reign forever and ever" (15:18) now say, "Would that we had died in Egypt" (16:3). The speed of this reversal — from cosmic hymn to death-wish in the space of a few verses — establishes the pattern that will govern the entire wilderness narrative: the distance between doxology and despair is measured in days, and hunger is enough to traverse it. The murmuring is not merely complaint but theological regression: the people prefer the predictable provision of slavery (sir habasar, "the pot of meat") to the uncertain provision of freedom. The pot of meat is a known quantity; the wilderness is not. The Exodus has liberated Israel's bodies but not yet their imaginations — they can conceive of abundance only in Egyptian categories.

God's response (vv. 4–5) reframes the entire situation. Instead of rebuke, God announces provision: "I am about to rain bread from heaven." The verb mamtir ("to cause to rain") places the manna within the meteorological language of divine sovereignty — the same God who sends rain for crops now sends bread from the sky. But the provision comes with conditions that transform it into pedagogy. The phrase devar-yom beyomo ("the matter of a day in its day") establishes a principle of radical dailiness: each day's bread is for that day only. The prohibition against hoarding (vv. 19–20) and the consequence of disobedience (worms and stench) teach Israel that security cannot be stored. Tomorrow's bread must come from tomorrow's God. This is not merely an economic arrangement but a spiritual discipline — the daily gathering of manna is the daily practice of faith.

The testing motif (lema'an anassenu, v. 4) reveals that provision and test are inseparable. God provides precisely in order to test: Will they walk in My torah or not? The word torah here (v. 4) and its plural torotay ("My instructions," v. 28) appear before Sinai — the giving of the law at Sinai is not the beginning of God's instruction but its formalization. Torah-as-instruction precedes Torah-as-document. The manna is itself a form of torah: it teaches dependence, contentment, and the rhythm of work and rest.

The Sabbath institution (vv. 22–30) emerges organically from the manna narrative. The double portion on the sixth day is not a human invention but a divine signal: the rhythm of the manna itself encodes the Sabbath. The phrase shabbaton shabbat-qodesh laYHWH (v. 23, "a rest, a holy Sabbath to the LORD") is the first Sabbath legislation in the Torah — it precedes the Decalogue by four chapters. The Sabbath is thus presented not as a legal imposition from Sinai but as a pattern embedded in creation and provision: the rhythm of gathering and resting, of six days' labor and one day's cessation, is written into the structure of the manna before it is written on the tablets of stone. The command shevu ish tachtav, al-yetse ish mimqomo bayyom hashevi'i ("remain each man in his place; let no man go out from his place on the seventh day," v. 29) defines Sabbath rest as remaining — staying in place, ceasing from the restless quest for more. The manna teaches Israel that enough is enough, and the Sabbath teaches that enough is given.

The miraculous equalization described in v. 18 — hammarbeh lo he'dif vehamma'it lo hechsir ("the one who gathered much had nothing over, and the one who gathered little had no lack") — is one of the most remarkable statements in the chapter. Regardless of how much or how little each person gathered, when measured by the omer, each had exactly enough. This divine equalization of provision is cited by Paul in 2 Corinthians 8:15 in the context of economic sharing among churches. The theological implication is that God's provision is inherently sufficient and just: it cannot be hoarded into surplus, and it does not diminish into deficit. The omer — exactly enough for one person for one day — is the measure of divine generosity, which is always precisely adequate.

The preservation of the manna-jar (vv. 32–34) transforms daily bread into permanent memorial. The command to fill a tsintseneth ("jar" — a word that occurs only here in the Bible) and place it lifney ha'Edut ("before the Testimony") creates a physical link between provision and covenant: the bread from heaven rests beside the words from heaven. The manna-jar, placed before the Tablets, becomes a perpetual witness that the God who speaks also feeds, that the God who commands also provides. Future generations (ledoroteikhem, "for your generations") will see the omer of manna and remember that their ancestors did not live by their own labor in the wilderness but by the daily, miraculous, inexplicable bread of God.

The chapter's concluding notes — the forty years of manna (v. 35) and the measure of the omer (v. 36) — provide the temporal and quantitative frame for the entire wilderness experience. Forty years of daily bread: this is approximately 14,600 mornings of gathering, 14,600 acts of trust, 14,600 renewals of dependence. The omer — a tenth of an ephah, roughly a handful — is the daily allotment of divine grace. It is not lavish by human standards; it is sufficient by divine standards. The manna teaches Israel, and through Israel teaches the world, that the God who redeems also sustains, and that sustenance is best received not in storehouses but in open hands, daily, one omer at a time.

The Vaticanus/LXX tradition adds one notable detail absent from the Hebrew: the tsintseneth ("jar") is rendered stamnon chrusoun ("a golden jar"). This interpretive addition — whether from an older Hebrew text, from Hellenistic liturgical tradition, or from the translator's theological imagination — transforms the humble wilderness container into a sacred vessel worthy of the Tabernacle's interior. The golden jar appears again in Hebrews 9:4, where it is listed alongside the budding staff of Aaron and the Tablets of the Covenant as the contents of the Ark. The LXX's golden jar represents a reading tradition in which the manna memorial is elevated from the functional to the liturgical — the humble bread of the wilderness, preserved in gold, testifies that what sustains the body is sacred enough to be housed in the holiest place.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 16 (pages 57–58) preserves the LXX version with several notable features. At v. 3, the LXX adds plēgentes ("struck, smitten") to the death-wish, making the people wish for death by divine plague rather than by mere death — a more theologically charged formulation. At v. 4, the LXX uses the present tense huō ("I rain") rather than the Hebrew's imminent participle mamtir ("about to rain"), flattening the temporal nuance. At v. 13, the LXX uses ortugometra for selav, the standard Greek term for the common quail. At v. 14, the LXX appears to conflate elements of vv. 14 and 31, describing the manna as "fine like white coriander" — an apparent harmonization. At v. 15, the LXX translates man hu as ti estin touto ("what is this?") rather than transliterating, making the etymology immediately accessible to Greek readers but losing the Aramaic/dialectal flavor. At v. 23, the LXX uses the plural sabbata for shabbat, reflecting the Greek tendency to use the transliterated plural form. At v. 29, the LXX adds hēmeran tautēn ("this day") to make the Sabbath reference temporally concrete. At v. 31, the LXX transliterates the name man rather than translating it. At v. 33, the LXX's stamnon chrusoun ("golden jar") for tsintseneth is the most consequential divergence — adding a material specification ("golden") that the Hebrew does not contain and that enters the Christian canon through Hebrews 9:4. At v. 36, the LXX interprets the ephah as "three measures" (tria metra), reflecting the equivalence of one ephah to three seahs, rather than simply naming the ephah as a unit. Throughout the Vaticanus pages, the text is written in the same continuous uncial script as the surrounding chapters, with no special formatting for the manna narrative.

--- LOGOS
